seoTitle: Post Adjusting Journal Entries in QuickBooks Online
metaDescription: Record month-end adjusting entries for accruals, prepaid expenses, and depreciation in QuickBooks Online using the manual journal tool.
intro: Closing out an accounting period cleanly requires adjusting entries for things like accruals, prepaid expenses, or depreciation, and getting the debits and credits balanced matters for an accurate audit trail. This guide covers recording an adjusting entry in QuickBooks Online correctly.

steps.heading: Open the Journal Entry form
steps.body: Click the + New button, then choose Journal Entry under the Other column.

steps.heading: Set the period-end date and reference
steps.body: Enter the official period-end date and assign a unique, sequential reference number.

steps.heading: Flag it as an adjusting entry
steps.body: Check the Is Adjusting Entry? toggle so the line is specifically marked for period closes.

steps.heading: Enter your debit and credit lines
steps.body: Choose your target account and enter the debit value on the first row, then select the offsetting account and enter the balancing credit on the second row.

steps.heading: Save and verify
steps.body: Confirm the totals row shows a perfect balance, click Save and Close, then run a fresh Trial Balance or Profit and Loss to verify the adjustment posted correctly.

faq.question: Why does QuickBooks require debits and credits to match exactly?
faq.answer: Double-entry accounting requires total debits to equal total credits before the form validation will let you save the entry.

faq.question: What does the Is Adjusting Entry toggle do?
faq.answer: It flags the transaction specifically as a period-close adjustment, giving your accountant clearer context during an audit.

faq.question: How do I confirm my adjusting entry posted correctly?
faq.answer: Run a fresh Trial Balance or Profit and Loss statement afterward to confirm the adjustment reflects accurately.

faq.question: Can I use this method for depreciation entries too?
faq.answer: Yes, depreciation adjustments follow the same manual journal entry process, mapping to the appropriate asset and expense accounts.
